# Maloha Coast — Brand Identity

## Finalised Brand Decisions (locked June 2026)

### Colour Palette
- **Primary**: #2a6a8a (teal blue)
- **Accent**: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- **Dark Navy**: #0a1628
- **Light backgrounds**: #f4f8fb, #ffffff

### Typography
- **Primary font**: Optima (humanist, elegant Roman forms)
- **Fallback stack**: 'Segoe UI', 'Candara', 'Palatino Linotype', 'Book Antiqua', Palatino, serif

### Brand Pattern
⬡ **Honeycomb Hexagon Grid** — Gold on Navy
- Hex lines: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- Background field: #0a1628 (dark navy)
- Tile size: 90×52 px seamless tiling PNG
- Hosted at: https://iili.io/CK3nHLF.png
- Also available in 7 other colour variants (see skill references)

## Email Templates

The production email template is based on the "Ocean Deep" direction (selected from 5 brand concepts A-E).

### Template Structure
1. **Outer wrapper**: Gold-on-navy hex pattern background (via HTML `background` attribute on `<td>`, NOT CSS)
2. **Header**: Pattern visible with rgba(10,22,40,0.65) semi-transparent overlay → "MALOHA COAST" in white + "Sdn Bhd" in gold
3. **Body card**: White rounded card with generous padding
4. **Info cards**: Gold left accent border (#c4a86a) on light teal (#f4f8fb) background
5. **Signature**: Gold horizontal rule (#c4a86a) + name/title/company/tagline in brand colours
6. **Footer**: Same hex pattern background as header

### Gmail Compatibility (verified)
- Use HTML `background` attribute on `<td>` elements for patterns (CSS `background-image` with data URIs is stripped by Gmail)
- Use nested `<table>` with `rgba()` overlay for colour tinting
- Use inline styles only (no `<style>` blocks)
- Max width 600px for email card
- No JavaScript

### Skill Reference
The full workflow is in the `html-email-with-sheet-logging` skill:
- **SKILL.md**: /home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/productivity/html-email-with-sheet-logging/SKILL.md
- **Production template**: references/ocean-deep-template.html
- **Header fragment**: templates/maloha-coast-header-fragment.html
- **Hex pattern variants**: references/hex-pattern-variants.md
- **Script to regenerate**: scripts/generate-hex-pattern.py

### Brand Concepts File
Full brand exploration HTML (5 palettes, 12 patterns, typography pairings):
/home/ubuntu/maloha-coast-brand-concepts.html

pages.body:
# Fwd: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Mon, 8 Jun 2026 23:49:48 +0800
**Source:** email

> Forwarded message from Nate Herk <nate@aiautomationsociety.ai>

June 05, 2026 — AI Automation Society Newsletter

## The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

Sharper Claude context, Carson's first automation win, and Anthropic's $965B IPO filing

### In This Issue
- The single technique that makes every Claude project sound less like everyone else and more like you
- Carson landed his first automation client with a free pilot — here's the exact move
- Anthropic just filed for IPO at $965 billion — what it means if Claude is your primary tool

### New Video: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

If your Claude outputs feel generic — technically correct but missing your voice — the problem is not the model. Everyone using Claude Opus 4.8 starts from the same foundation. The difference between outputs that sound like you and ones that sound like everyone else is context.

**The Grill Me skill** fixes that. Instead of a five-minute brain dump before you start building, it interviews you until it actually understands how you think. It asks one question at a time, checkpoints your answers to a markdown file in your project, and keeps going until there are no gaps. By the end, you have reusable context your AI pulls from every time you touch that skill or project.

**The mistake most people make:** Starting with a brain dump and calling it good enough. Five minutes of info-dumping gets your output quality to about 70% on iteration one. The Grill Me approach gets you to 90% from the start — fewer revision cycles, faster time to a deliverable clients actually like.

**How this helps you:** When Claude knows your voice, your decisions, and your process, it stops generating generic output. Your projects feel like yours. Clients notice. You spend less time fixing what the AI got wrong.

### Winning Is All We Do — [[Carson]] landed his first automation client

**What happened:** [[Carson]] built a lead automation for a local aesthetics clinic as a free pilot. The workflow routes contact data and handles follow-ups automatically. He's waiting on Facebook credentials to go live, but the clinic owner is already highly receptive. His plan: deliver the pilot, then position for a paid monthly retainer.

**The opportunity:** Local service businesses (clinics, salons, studios) are some of the easiest first clients to land. They have real lead problems, a low technology bar, and if you deliver, they talk to other business owners. A free pilot with a clear upsell path is one of the cleanest ways to get your first paying automation client.

### In the News — [[Anthropic]] filed for IPO at $965 billion

**The news:** On June 1, 2026, [[Anthropic]] — the company behind Claude — confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a proposed initial public offering. Valuation: $965 billion, putting it ahead of [[OpenAI]] ($852 billion after their last funding round). [[Anthropic]] joins SpaceX and [[OpenAI]] in what analysts are already calling a race to go public — all three expected to list this year.

**How it affects automation:** When your primary AI tool's parent company goes public, two things typically follow: more capital to build better models (good for you), and quarterly earnings pressure that tends to push enterprise-first roadmaps and price adjustments upward. This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to build your skills and client base now, before the landscape shifts again.

### AIS Live — July 11-12, 2026

[[AI Automation Society]] hosts a two-day event where real practitioners walk through real client engagements on stage. Day 1: see the exact projects being sold right now and build one live. Day 2: exactly how to get clients, even if you're a beginner with no credibility yet. General Admission starts at $97 (early bird ends June 7).

### People Mentioned
- [[Nate Herk]] — Author of the original newsletter from [[AI Automation Society]]
- [[Carson]] — Practitioner who landed first automation client

### Companies Mentioned
- [[AI Automation Society]] — Publisher
- [[Anthropic]] — Filed IPO at $965B
- [[OpenAI]] — $852B valuation

---

pages.title: Company Memory Os Ph1 Prd
pages.body:
# Hermes Agent to ingest
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Tue, 9 Jun 2026 10:50:14 +0800
**Source:** email forward

Here's a compact PRD designed specifically for Hermes to execute against.

PRD: Company Memory OS (Phase 1)

## Author

Authored by [[Sayf Codes]].

## Vision

Build a Company Memory Operating System that transforms fragmented
communication into a searchable institutional memory.

Initial delivery is service-first, software-second.

The goal is not to sell software access.

The goal is to deliver the outcome:
"Never lose important decisions, context, relationships, or knowledge
again."

---

## Problem

Small businesses, consultants, agencies, and founders lose critical
knowledge because it is scattered across:

- Email
- Meeting transcripts
- Documents
- Chat conversations
- Notes

Knowledge becomes unrecoverable after weeks or months.

Current solutions focus on storage.

Users need retrieval and context.

---

## Target Customer

### Primary — Consultants

Characteristics:
- 20-100 clients
- High email volume
- Multiple projects running simultaneously
- Knowledge-intensive work
- Decisions buried in communications

### Secondary
- Agency owners
- Startup founders
- Fractional executives
- Professional services firms

---

## Core Value Proposition

Transform company communications into a searchable memory system.

**Input:** Emails, Meeting transcripts, Documents

**Output:** Searchable knowledge base, Decision history, Relationship intelligence, Project memory, AI-assisted retrieval

---

## Initial Offer

Productized Service — Not SaaS. Human-assisted onboarding.

### Knowledge Audit

**Customer provides:** Emails, Documents, Meeting notes

**System generates:** Executive summary, Project map, Key decisions, Action items, Stakeholder map, Recurring themes, Knowledge gaps

**Deliverable:** Interactive dashboard + report.

---

## Success Metric

Customer says: *"I found something valuable that I would not have found otherwise."*

---

## MVP Scope

### Ingestion
Support: Email forwarding, Gmail export, Meeting transcripts, Markdown documents, PDFs

### Processing
Extract: People, Companies, Projects, Decisions, Tasks, Dates, Relationships
Generate: Summaries, Tags, Backlinks, Knowledge graph connections

### Retrieval
User can ask:
- What was decided about Project X?
- What commitments were made to Client Y?
- Show all discussions involving Person Z.
- What unresolved action items exist?

### Dashboard
Views: Decision Timeline, Relationship Graph, Project Knowledge Base, Open Actions, Search (natural language)

---

## Go-To-Market

**Goal:** Acquire first 3 paying customers.

**Offer:** Free Knowledge Audit

**Target:** Existing network, Founders, Consultants, Agency owners

**Message:** *"I've built a system that turns emails, meetings, and documents into a searchable company memory. Looking for 3 companies to test it with."*

---

## Validation Questions

1. What was most valuable?
2. What surprised you?
3. What would you pay monthly to keep this updated?
4. Which feature would you miss most?
5. Would you recommend this to someone else?

---

## Pricing Experiments

| Stage | Service | Price |
|-------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Knowledge Audit (one-time) | RM300 - RM1000 |
| 2 | Continuous Memory Service (monthly) | RM300 - RM1000/month |
| 3 | SaaS | Only after 10+ paying customers |

---

## Strategic Principle

Do not optimize for software. Optimize for customer outcomes.

Use Hermes + GBrain internally as operational infrastructure. The platform emerges from repeated customer success, not from building features first.

---

## North Star

Become the memory layer for small organizations.

Long-term vision: Communication → Knowledge Graph → Institutional Memory → Organizational Intelligence

pages.title: Knowledge Graph As Moat
pages.body:
# Knowledge Graph as Moat

**Synthesized:** 2026-06-09
**Sources:** Graphify video (Jack, YouTube), email-to-gbrain pipeline benchmarks, Company Memory OS PRD

## The Pattern

The same architectural pattern works across two different domains:

| Layer | Code Domain (Graphify) | Business Domain (Company Memory OS / gbrain) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ingest** | Clone GitHub repo | Forward email, upload transcript |
| **Parse** | Read code, cluster into modules | Extract entities, detect relationships |
| **Graph** | Files → modules → god nodes | People → companies → deals → decisions |
| **Query** | "What depends on X?" | "What was decided about Project X?" |
| **Dashboard** | Interactive circle graph | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| **Agent** | Claude Code + Hermes | Hermes + gbrain |
| **Compounding** | Every session builds context | Every email ingested enriches memory |

## Core Insight

Both systems solve the same fundamental problem: **"rereading is the tax."**

- In code: re-reading the repo every conversation costs tokens and produces shallower answers
- In business: re-searching emails/meetings every time you need context costs time and lets decisions fall through cracks

The knowledge graph IS the moat. Once a codebase or a business has been mapped:
- Answers come from summaries, not re-parsing
- Dependencies and blast radius are visible before making changes  
- Context compounds across sessions
- Switching costs increase dramatically — the graph IS the switching cost

## Why This Validates Company Memory OS

Jack's video pitches Graphify as worth paying for because it saves Claude Code users money and produces better results. The same value proposition applies to Company Memory OS:

- For a consultant with 50 clients: "What commitments did I make to Client Y?" answered in <500ms
- For an agency owner: "Show me all discussions about the Q2 budget" without digging through email
- For a founder: "Who did I meet at the June conference and what did we discuss?"

The architecture is proven. The pattern is validated. The question is execution and positioning.

## Key References

- [[media/videos/graphify-solves-claude-code-problem]] — Full video analysis with chapter index, quotes, and architecture breakdown
- [[concepts/company-memory-os-ph1-prd]] — Phase 1 PRD for the Company Memory OS productized service
- The email-to-gbrain pipeline (cron job `776c7bf32c77`) is the working MVP of Phase 1

## Client Pitch Analogy

"What Graphify does for code — instant understanding of any codebase without reading through it — we do for your business communications. Forward an email. Get a searchable memory. Never lose a decision again."

pages.title: Openclaw Anatomy
pages.body:
# Fwd: The Anatomy of OpenClaw
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Tue, 9 Jun 2026 00:11:45 +0800
**Source:** email forward

Forwarded from **[[Neo Kim]]** ([[System Design One]]) — Issue #151.

> **Understanding How OpenClaw Works**

OpenClaw is an open-source autonomous AI agent that runs on your machine, connects to messaging apps, and executes tasks directly — no manual copy-paste needed. It bridges the "thinking layer" (LLMs like Claude, GPT, Gemini) and the "act layer" (tools, APIs, files).

## What OpenClaw Offers

- **Gateway**: Always-on Node.js server (port 18789) coordinating messaging apps, LLM, tools, and memory. Runs as a background daemon — persistence is the foundation of scheduled jobs, proactive alerts, and multi-channel messaging.
- **Multi-channel messaging**: WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack — shared context and memory across all platforms.
- **Skills platform (ClawHub, 5,400+ capabilities)**: Plain Markdown files that define specific tasks (check GitHub PRs, send Slack messages).
- **Persistent memory**: Markdown files on disk — inspectable, version-controllable.
- **Proactive scheduling**: Heartbeat + cron jobs that act without being prompted.
- **Model-agnostic**: Claude, GPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, Grok, local via Ollama — mix and match.

## How It Differs

- ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini: browser-only, no access to email/scripts, no persistence.
- Siri/Google Assistant: closed products, no custom skills or self-hosting.
- LangChain/CrewAI/AutoGen: developer frameworks requiring Python code.
- Claude Code/Codex CLI: terminal tools for coding sessions — not 24/7.
- **OpenClaw runs 24/7** for everything, not just code.

## Architecture Layers

**Gateway** (Control Center): Orchestrates everything — authenticates senders, assembles context, passes to LLM, handles tool calls, routes replies. Sends command confirmation requests (e.g., `rm -rf ~/logs/*.log` needs approval). Serves Control UI at `http://localhost:18789` for real-time monitoring. Supports hot configuration reloads.

**Channel Layer**: Translation adapters per platform — Telegram (bot token + polling/webhook), WhatsApp (reverse-engineered web protocol, QR auth), Slack (Socket Mode, two token types), Discord (gateway WebSocket). Normalizes messages into internal format. Security policies (allowlists, pairing) apply uniformly.

**LLM Layer**: Assembles system prompt from agent personality + available skills + retrieved memory + conversation history. Calls the AI model. Tool calls execute and feed results back — loop continues until final response. Compaction triggers at context limit (summarizes history).

**Plugin System**: Almost everything non-core is a plugin — Telegram, GitHub, memory. Plugins hook into lifecycle events (before/after tool calls, messages, session start/end). Enables audit logging, rate limiting, custom approval flows without touching core.

**Memory System**: Three-layer approach — (1) short-term: session files on disk, loads recent context only; (2) long-term: all sessions indexed into local database, auto-searches before answering; (3) standing notes: Markdown files in `~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/` as permanent briefing documents.

**Long-running tasks**: Agent acknowledges task, works in background, messages when done. State written to disk, not held in memory. Cron jobs in isolated session mode for truly long-running work.

## Key Insights

- The Gateway runs on your Mac Mini or VPS — accessible via Tailscale from any device.
- Control UI shows every tool call, model response, and approval request in real time.
- Model can be changed at any time without reconfiguration.
- If a single tool call takes too long, Gateway times out — break large tasks into chunks.
- DeepResearch runs long sessions but stops when done — OpenClaw never stops.

*This was a preview of a subscriber-only post from Neo Kim's System Design Newsletter.*

pages.body:
# The 3-Folder System: Atlas → Projects → End Products (Head, Hands, Heart)

**Source:** YouTube — "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture"
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rl7rvHbDqGk
**Format:** Video transcript
**Ingested:** 2026-06-05

## Summary

The speaker discovered that disorganized AI outputs were a reflection of disorganized thinking. After months of building AI agents and burning API tokens, he stopped and realized the real problem wasn't the tools — it was his cognitive architecture. His core insight: **your file structure is a map of your thinking**. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar.

The solution is embarrassingly simple: a three-folder system that maps to Head, Hands, and Heart.

## The Three-Folder Framework

### 1. Atlas (Head / Thinking)
Raw materials, ideas, strategy, capture. Where you put ideas when you get them — the thinking layer. Everything that isn't yet a project.

### 2. Projects (Hands / Building)
Active work. Content being created, things being built. This is the doing layer.

### 3. End Products (Heart / Serving)
Completed work. Finished deliverables that serve other people. The output layer.

## Key Insights

- **File structure = cognitive architecture.** Your external files reveal your internal thinking patterns.
- **Organization is speed.** [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor): "Organization is heaven's first law." Clear structure makes AI faster and cheaper (fewer tokens).
- **The sophistication trap.** Smart people overcomplicate things. The answer is usually simpler than you think.
- **AI reveals you to yourself.** AI doesn't just do thinking FOR you — it shows you HOW you think by externalizing your patterns.
- **Agents mirror human growth.** An AI agent needs identity, context, and skills — the same three things a human needs to change their life.
- **Stop building, start organizing.** Before chasing the next tool or agent, fix the foundation.

## Claude Mirrors the Same Structure

| Mode | Purpose | Maps To |
|------|---------|---------|
| Chat | Thinking, exploring ideas | Atlas (Head) |
| Co-work | Building artifacts, plans | Projects (Hands) |
| Claude Code | Finished products, shipped work | End Products (Heart) |

## The Three Communities Pattern

The speaker's Skool communities also follow this structure:
- **Free community** (Lifestyle Founders Group) → Thinking, sharing ideas (Atlas)
- **Paid community** (Claude for Coaches) → Active building, workshops (Projects)
- **VIP community** → End products, completed work (Heart)

## Notable Quotes

- "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar."
- "Organization is heaven's first law." — [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor)
- "The bottleneck isn't the tool, it's the clarity of your thinking. Your cognitive architecture is now your competitive advantage."
- "Fix the inside, the outside follows. As above, so below."
- "I need a second brain sounds nice, but actually my first brain needs to get organized first."

## People/Resources Mentioned

- [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor) — "Organization is heaven's first law"
- [Jake Van Cleef](people/jake-van-cleef) — academic paper on stopping agent building, fixing file structure first
- [Nick Milo](people/nick-milo) — Obsidian/second brain advocate, three-folder patterns
- Head, Hands & Heart Notes: https://imported-abacus-8dd.notion.site/
- Free Skool Community: https://skool.com/lfg
- Claude For Coaches: https://www.skool.com/claudeforcoaches/about
- Lifestyle Founder Mastermind: https://www.skool.com/quantum100/about

pages.title: Virtual Machines Deep Dive
pages.body:
# How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive (#148: Part 1 - DevOps Mastermind)

**From:** [[Neo Kim]] (via systemdesignone@substack.com, forwarded by Sayf Codes)
**Date:** Mon, 25 May 2026
**Source:** email forward

---

**DevOps Mastermind** — A newsletter series to help engineers master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing commands. Architecture breakdowns of core DevOps systems, deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and practical insights into scalability, automation, and reliability.

---

## Emulation vs Virtualization

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware (e.g., retro gaming emulators). Each instruction from the old system is translated for the modern chip. It works, but it's slow.

Virtualization creates a version of the same hardware the machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor. Result: near-native speed.

- Emulation = hiring a translator for every sentence
- Virtualization = both people already speak the same language

This speed is what made virtualization practical at scale — you cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a single server. With virtualization, you can.

## What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A VM is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine. It's presented with CPU, memory, storage, and network — just like a physical system. The OS inside has no awareness it's sharing the machine.

**The hypervisor** is the layer that makes the illusion possible. Every request for CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network goes through it. Three key capabilities:

1. **Isolation** — each VM runs independently; a failure in one doesn't affect others
2. **Portability** — VMs can move between physical systems with minimal changes
3. **Efficient resource use** — multiple workloads share the same hardware

## Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion

### Type 1 vs Type 2

| Type | Description | Examples |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Type 1 (bare-metal)** | Runs directly on physical hardware, no host OS in between | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM |
| **Type 2 (hosted)** | Runs on top of an existing OS like a regular app | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

### Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

- **Full virtualization**: Guest OS runs unmodified; hypervisor handles all abstraction work
- **Paravirtualization**: Guest OS is aware it's virtualized and cooperates with the hypervisor to reduce overhead
- Trade-off: compatibility (full) vs performance (para)

### Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Intel VT-x and AMD-V let the CPU itself differentiate between VMs and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor automatically. This made virtualization practical at scale.

### Nested Virtualization

Running a VM inside another VM. Useful for testing and local Kubernetes setups but unsuitable for production due to overhead.

## CPU Virtualization

### The Privilege Model

In a normal system, the OS sits at the highest privilege level. In a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the OS. The guest OS gets moved down a level — but never knows it was demoted.

When the guest tries a privileged operation, the processor hands control to the hypervisor.

### vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a VM, you assign vCPUs (virtual CPUs). The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores via **time-slicing** — the same way an OS runs multiple apps.

**CPU overcommit**: Assigning more vCPUs than physical cores (e.g., 16 cores running 32 vCPUs). Works if all VMs aren't busy simultaneously. When demand rises, contention reduces performance.

### VM-exit and VM-entry

- **VM-exit**: VM tries a privileged instruction → processor stops it → control transfers to hypervisor
- **VM-entry**: Hypervisor handles the request → hands control back to the VM exactly where it left off
- Handoff takes microseconds. But frequent exits hurt performance.

### NUMA Awareness (Non-Uniform Memory Access)

Modern servers have multiple processors, each with local memory. Accessing memory on a different processor is slower. The hypervisor tries to keep a VM's vCPUs and memory on the same NUMA node.

For most small deployments this doesn't matter. For high-performance databases on large servers, it matters a lot.

---

|*This was a free preview of a subscriber-only post from [[Neo Kim]]'s "The System Design Newsletter". Guest author: [[Ayaan]] (self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure).*

## Key Takeaways

- Virtualization enabled cloud computing by splitting one powerful machine into many isolated ones
- The hypervisor is the core abstraction layer — handles CPU, memory, storage, and networking
- Hardware support (VT-x/AMD-V) made virtualization practical at scale
- Containers are a different approach (OS-level virtualization, shared kernel) — covered in Part 1's comparison

## People Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — Author of The System Design Newsletter (systemdesignone.substack.com). Wrote this deep dive on virtualization architecture as part of the DevOps Mastermind series.
- **[[Ayaan]]** — Self-taught DevOps engineer, guest author. Focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure. Builds in public on Twitter/X and GitHub.

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes One Afternoon
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb960b3c2b3ec2
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12T09:09:30Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-12

---

Sayf's note: newsletter shared

---

**From:** [[Adrian (JS Mastery)]] | [[JS Mastery]]
**Subject:** Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.
**Date:** Thu, 11 Jun 2026

February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing. This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified. Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge. Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
[[Adrian (JS Mastery)]]
[[JS Mastery]]

# Zaim Mohzani

> **Role:** AI & Career Strategist | ASEAN Policy Specialist | Senior Director at KSI
> **Location:** Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
> **LinkedIn:** https://my.linkedin.com/in/zaimmohzani
> **Website:** www.zaim.my
> **1st-degree connection with sayf** [Source: User, LinkedIn, 2026-06-09]

---

## Compilation of Facts (Compiled Truth)

Zaim Mohzani is a Malaysian career strategist, ASEAN policy specialist, and social entrepreneur. He helps mid-career professionals change careers without starting over, using a lifestyle-centric career design approach. He has directly helped 2,600+ professionals in Malaysia since 2015. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

He is Malaysia's first certified Designing Your Life (DYL) coach, trained by Bill Burnett and Dave Evans at Stanford University. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### Core Philosophy: Lifestyle-Centric Career Design

Zaim's approach rejects the "follow your passion" advice. Instead:
- Start with the lifestyle you actually want
- Work backwards to careers that support it
- Build skills with market value
- Make moves based on reality, not feelings

His key thesis: **Passion is a by-product of doing good work, not a prerequisite.** [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

### Personal Story

At 25, Zaim had strong credentials but was burnt out — hundreds of applications, zero offers. He was told "you just haven't found your passion yet." That advice kept him paralysed. He pivoted through five industries and at 28 became chairman of a Malaysian government agency — not because he found his calling, but because he stopped chasing passion and started designing for the life he wanted. [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

---

[Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

## Organizations Founded / Led

Social enterprise started as a bootstrapped NGO in Bukit Tunku, now empowering next-generation leaders. Has engaged over 10,000 youths in Malaysia and Southeast Asia. Focus: career workshops and leadership programmes for urban poor, single mothers, and youth at risk. During the pandemic, launched virtual career workshops. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

pages.title: Cold Lead Response Conference Events
pages.body:
**Context:** Response to a conference producer who asked for examples after a cold outreach. Crafted for LinkedIn DM — punchy, concrete, pilot-friendly.

## The Response

Thanks for asking. Here are some practical ways I think a Hermes agent could fit into your events workflow if we set one up on your laptop or VPS:

**Speaker sourcing:** You feed it your target criteria once, and it scans hundreds of profiles, past talks, and decks. It comes back with a shortlist plus solid briefing notes in about 15 minutes instead of 3–4 hours of manual digging.

**Post-session repurposing:** Right after a talk, you drop in the recording or transcript and it generates a full LinkedIn article, 3-5 social posts, a newsletter summary, and key highlights. Turns one session into 30+ usable pieces.

**Sponsor deliverables:** It pulls from your existing docs and data to create pre-event copy, staff briefs, lead reports, and post-event ROI summaries — cutting out a big chunk of the 20 hours per sponsor tier your team usually spends.

**Business brain / company memory:** I'm actually building one of these for myself right now. We can set up a "gbrain" where the agent learns your past events, speakers, sponsors, what worked and what didn't. Then you can just ask it things like "What sponsors performed best last year?" or "Give me the checklist for a 500-person event" and it pulls from your own history.

**Closer:** Which one sounds like it would save you the most time or headache right now? Happy to walk through any of them in more detail or hop on a quick call to map it to how you currently work.

## Notes

- Crafted to be realistic, specific, and pilot-friendly
- No AI jargon — pitched as a tool they control
- Each example maps to a known pain point for conference producers
- The gbrain example doubles as a subtle demo of the product in use

pages.title: Vps Hardening
pages.body:
# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

## Related
- [[Google Workspace Setup]]
- [[2026-06-03]]
- [[Gateway Infrastructure]] — gateway health monitoring and watchdog
